AtomicFact.description: A single atomic fact extracted from a source.

direct_quote.description: Exact quote from source supporting this fact
direct_quote.title: Direct Quote

fact_category.description: Category: statistic, measurement, causal_link, named_entity, date_time, regulatory_threshold, standard_reference, geographic, methodology
fact_category.title: Fact Category

relevance_score.description: Relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
relevance_score.title: Relevance Score

confidence.description: Confidence that this fact is correctly extracted, 0.0 to 1.0
confidence.title: Confidence

perspective.description: STORM research perspective: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends
perspective.title: Perspective

entities.description: Key named entities (compounds, methods, organizations, metrics) mentioned in this fact. Used by the wiki mesh entity canonicalization pass. Optional; legacy data defaults to [].
entities.title: Entities

AtomicFact.title: AtomicFact

SourceAnalysis.description: Analysis of a single source document.

source_type.description: Type: journal_article, government_report, industry_report, news, standard, patent, book, other
source_type.title: Source Type

overall_relevance.description: Overall relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
overall_relevance.title: Overall Relevance

atomic_facts.description: All atomic facts extracted from this source
atomic_facts.title: Atomic Facts

evidence_summary.description: Brief summary of what this source contributes
evidence_summary.title: Evidence Summary

SourceAnalysis.title: SourceAnalysis

system:
You are a research evidence analyst. Extract atomic facts from source material.

Rules:
1. Extract 8-15 atomic facts per source. Focus on the MOST relevant, specific, and unique claims, statistics, findings, and conclusions. Quality over quantity — prioritize facts that DIRECTLY answer the research question over tangential details. Missing a highly relevant fact is worse than including marginally relevant ones.
   CRITICAL: ONLY extract from the ARTICLE BODY TEXT. NEVER extract from: navigation menus, cookie consent banners, URL paths/slugs, abbreviation/glossary tables, author byline metadata, page footers/headers, table of contents page numbers, product category labels, or marketing taglines. If the source is mostly non-content (nav, ads, cookie text), extract ZERO facts and set source_quality to 0.0.
2. Each atomic fact = ONE specific claim with the EXACT quote from the source (minimum 10 words, up to 200 chars). A quote shorter than 5 words is NEVER acceptable — skip that fact entirely.
3. Source quality: Score each source individually on 0.0-1.0 based on:
   - Peer review status (peer-reviewed journal > preprint > report > blog)
   - Author credentials (named experts > organizations > anonymous)
   - Recency (2023-2026 > 2018-2022 > older)
   - Specificity to research question (directly relevant > tangentially related)
   Do NOT use fixed scores for source types. Vary scores based on each source's specific qualities.
4. Rate relevance to the SPECIFIC research question on 0.0-1.0:
   - 0.9-1.0: Directly answers part of the research question with specific data
   - 0.7-0.8: Provides important context or supporting evidence
   - 0.5-0.6: Tangentially relevant or provides background
   - 0.1-0.4: Marginally relevant, mostly off-topic
   Each fact must get a UNIQUE relevance score. No two facts from the same source should have identical scores.
5. Include exact numbers, dates, measurements, names, standards.
6. For EVERY quantitative finding, extract ALL of the following when available:
   - Effect size with units (MD, SMD, WMD, OR, HR, RR)
   - 95% confidence interval (e.g., "95% CI -5.54 to -3.05")
   - Heterogeneity (I-squared value, e.g., "I²=96%")
   - Number of studies/trials included (e.g., "7 RCTs")
   - Total sample size (e.g., "n=269")
   - Evidence certainty/quality rating (e.g., "GRADE: moderate", "high certainty")
   - Study duration/follow-up period
   Missing any of these when the source provides them is a CRITICAL extraction failure.
7. For review/meta-analysis papers, extract findings from EACH cited study individually.
   Prioritize pooled estimates with I² and CI over narrative summaries.
8. For each atomic fact, tag the research perspective it belongs to.
9. CRITICAL (ARCH-4): The 'statement' field must be a DIRECT PARAPHRASE of the source text.
   Do NOT infer, extrapolate, or add information beyond what the direct_quote states.
   If the direct_quote is fewer than 10 words, the statement must be equally narrow in scope.
10. QUOTE LENGTH: The 'direct_quote' field MUST be 150 characters or fewer.
   Quote only the KEY phrase, not entire sentences or paragraphs.
   This forces you to identify the most important fragment of evidence.
   BAD: "A systematic review and meta-analysis of 24 randomized controlled trials totaling 1,768 participants found that alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   GOOD: "alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   A 2-word quote like 'distilled water' can only support a statement about distilled water,
   not a full sentence about healthcare settings or bacterial contamination.
   Perspectives: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends.
   Choose the single best-fit perspective.

Output format (return ONLY this JSON structure):
{"analyses": [{"source_url": "https://example.com", "source_title": "Study Title", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.8, "overall_relevance": 0.7, "year": 2024, "authors": ["Smith J"], "venue": "Water Research", "doi": "", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "E. coli was detected in 30% of tested filters after 6 months", "direct_quote": "E. coli presence in 30% of tested POU filters", "fact_category": "statistic", "relevance_score": 0.9, "confidence": 0.8, "perspective": "Scientific"}], "evidence_summary": "Bacterial contamination in POU water filters"}]}
prompt:
Research question: How does damp heat aging affect the adhesion strength of DVS-PEI encapsulants in flexible electronics?

Analyze the following 2 sources and extract the TOP 8-15 MOST
relevant atomic facts per source. Focus on the most specific, unique, and
directly relevant numbers, dates, measurements, claims, findings,
regulations, and standards. Quality over quantity.

Sources:
Source URL: https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1887551
Source title: 
Source type: web
Content:
Title: The Natural and Accelerated Evolution of EVA Adhesion Through Intermediate Exposures

URL Source: https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1887551

Number of Pages: 6

Markdown Content:
The Natural and Accelerated Evolution of EVA 

# Adhesion through Intermediate Exposures 

Patrick Thornton , Student Member, IEEE , Nick Bosco , and Reinhold H. Dauskardt 

Abstract —Ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA ) encapsulants comprise 

the majority of the encapsulants currently in use ; much work has 

been done to understand and model the adhesive characteristics of 

EVA -encapsulated modules, but limited work has provided 

reliable insight into adhesion during the intermediate stages of 

exposur e, limiting the ability to validate model predictions in this 

range. Here we provide the adhesion energy measurements for 

EVA adhesion after nearly 6 years of field aging and 10,000 hours 

of accelerated agi ng. Both field and accelerated aging reveal a 

distinct plateau that emerges during the intermediate exposure 

periods (after 1 year in the field and after 1,000 hours in a 

chamber). At 10,000 hours, adhesion within accelerated aged 

mini -modules falls to a l evel generally seen after long -term field 

exposure s (>15 years). Previous model ing predicted that adhesion 

would steadily decrease over the lifetime of a module, but these 

current results uncover an intermediate plateauing trend that is 

important to accura tely modeling the evolution of adhesion and 

predicting adhesive failure. Based on these findings , three key 

model refinements concerning the rate of UV -radical formation 

and subsequent ß -scission, the rate and acceleration of hydrolytic 

depolymerization, and the profile of the plasticity contribution 

over time are implemented and discussed .

Keywords —adhesion , EVA , field / accelerated agin g, modeling 

I. INTRODUCTION 

Encapsulation plays a fundamental role in all photovoltaic 

(PV) modules. Not only does it physica lly and electrically 

isolate the solar cell within , but it also adhesive ly couples the 

outermost module layers (glass and/or polymer backsheets) to 

the solar cell . Ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA) has predominately 

fulfilled solar encapsulant needs since the 1980s [1] on account 

of its adequate material properties, including adhesion, 

combined with its affordability [2] . However, with extensive 

exposure to heat, humidity, and UV radiation comes chemical 

degradation [3] , which reduces its adhesive strength . This can 

ultimately lead to delamination failure of the module. 

To understand the evolution of adhesion within EVA -

encapsulated modules, studies hav e offered controlled 

investigations that are mostly confined to short exposures ,

which are obviously limited in their ability to inform on how 

adhesion loss progresses throughout a module’s lifetime [4] –

Th is work was authored in part by the National Renewable Energy 

Laboratory, operated by Alliance for Sustainable Energy, LLC, for the U.S .

Department of Energy (DOE) under Contract No. DE -AC36 -08GO28308. 

Funding provided by U.S. Department of Energy Office of Energy Efficiency 

and Renewable Energy Solar Energy Technologies Office. Corresponding 

author: R. H. Dauskardt 

Patrick Thornton is w ith Stanford University, Stanford, CA 94305 USA 

(email: pthornto@stanford.edu). 

[7] . Conversely, other non -controlled studies have provided 

insights into the final state of adhesion in modules that have 

been in the field for long periods; however, due to the lack of a 

control , the limitations of having only a singular , ultimate data 

point, and variations in material utilized across module 

manufacturers , these observational studies remain limited in 

their ability to detail how the evolution evolved over years of 

exposure [8] –[10] .

Here we investigate the evolution of adhesion in EVA -

encapsula ted mini -modules in a controlled study that includes 

specimens aged for 10,000 hours in accelerated conditions and 

specimens exposed in three different outdoor locations 

(Arizona, Colorado, and Florida) for nearly 6 years. The length 

of these controlled exposures provides reliable data for 

underexplored intermediate times . The adhesion energ y was 

measured using a fracture mechanics -based , width -tapered 

cantilever beam test , which yields a material property , allowing 

a more fundamental and quantitative assessment of adhesion .

The results add much -needed insight into the intermediate 

evolution of adhesive degradation and the path the adhesion 

loss takes from pristine to delaminated, which is an especially 

crucial consideration for efforts seeking to model and predict 

adhesion over the course of a module’s lifetime [10], [11] .

Recently, we developed a model framework to describe the 

molecular events that result in adhesive degradation within 

EVA [10] . It demonstrated a strong ability to describe both 

short -term (<1 year) a nd long -term (>15 years) data ; however, 

without any natural or accelerated results in the intermediate 

range, a prediction of a continuous decrease emerged here that 

remained unverifiable because of the lack of intermediate data. 

With the data and results from the current work, a trend emerges 

over intermediate exposure time s, providing the basis to refine 

the model parameters. The refinement s include adjusting the 

ra tes of the relevant degradation mechanisms (UV -radical 

formation/ß -scission and hydrolytic depolymerization) as well 

as updating the initial, simplifying assumption regarding the 

underlying contribution associated with non -linear plasticity ,

ultimately all owing the model to more accurately capture the 

adhesion trends demonstrated in both field and accelerated 

aging. This approach to characterizing and modeling adhesion 

is valuable to encapsulants beyond EV A since specific 

Nick Bosco is with the National Renewable Energy Laboratory, Golden, CO 

80401 USA (email: nick.bosco@nrel.gov). 

Reinhold H. Dauskardt is with the Materials Science and Engineering 

Department, Stanford University, Stanford, CA 94305 USA (email: 

dauskardt@stanford. edu ). Pursuant to the DOE Public Access Plan, this document represents the authors' peer-reviewed, accepted manuscript. The published version of the article is available from the relevant publisher. degradation mechanisms can be incor porated or removed as 

needed, on account of the fundamental and modular nature of 

the model. For example, with polyolefin elastomer (POE) 

encapsulants that are rapidly increasing in market share, the 

deacetylation reaction would no longer be applicable, but the 

UV -radical formation, ß-scission, and hydrolytic 

depolymerization reactions would be, and the model could be 

adjusted accordingly to account for this. 

II. EXPERIMENTAL DETAILS 

A. Mini -Module Preparation 

Single cell (150 mm x 150 mm) PV m ini -modules were 

encapsulated with EVA (33% vinyl acetate by mass, 14% 

molar, PhotoCap 15585P UF/HLT, Specialized Technology 

Resources, Inc.) between a 3 mm borosilicate front glass and a 

Kynar®/ PETE /EV A backsheet . Indoor aging consisted of a 

‘light’ and a ‘dark’ condition: light’ – 65˚C and 30% relative 

humidity (RH) under 340 nm UV light (Atlas Ci4000 Weather -

Ometer®, 81 W/m2, ~1.4x UV suns), and ‘dark’ – 85˚C and 

13.5% RH without UV light (Blue M, Mod el AC -7620HB 

chamber). The ‘light’ condition parameters were selected to 

compensate for heating effects from the UV light, allowing for 

accurate comparisons between the two sets of conditions. 

Outdoor field aging occurred at 3 locations: Golden, Colorado 

(CO); Miami, Florida (FL); and Phoenix, Arizona (AZ). 

B. Adhesion Testing 

Width -tapered cantilever beam tests were used to 

characterize the front encapsulant adhesion [12], [13] and 

measure the critical strain energy release r ate, or adhesion 

energy, Gc [J/m 2]. First, the backsheet and rear encapsulant 

layers are mechanically removed before a triangular beam 

specimen (Ti, 6% Al, 4% V, 20° apex angle) is adhered to the 

rear side of the solar cell with a two -part epoxy (DP420 or 

DP8005 -black , 3M) . A load applied with a strain rate of 10 

μm/s is placed at the apex of the triangle, which propagat es a

crack along the weakest interface at a critical load, Pc. The taper 

in the beam allows the crack to propagate at a constant load . Gc

is evaluated with (1) , usin g the critical load and a measurement 

of the final crack length, a. The load line displacement at the 

beam tip at the point of measurement is Δ, and θ is the beam 

taper angle. 

𝐺 𝑐 = 𝑃 𝑐 ∆

2𝑎 2 tan (θ/2) (1) 

In this work, several crack lengths and associated critical 

loads are obtained by inspection through the front glass ; thus ,

the Gc values and error represent the mean value and standard 

deviation of at least three measurements. 

III. ANALYTICAL ADHESION MODEL 

The original analytical model referenced throughout and 

refined within this work is described in detail elsewhere [10] ;

however, an overview is included here to provide a basis for the 

refinements . The model attribute d the overall adhesion e nergy 

Gc(t) [J/m 2] at a given time t [hours] to the sum of the intrinsic 

adhesion energy Gc,int,i (t) and contributions from localized 

plasticity Gc,PL (t) , given by 

𝐺 𝑐 (𝑡 ) = ∑ 𝐺 𝑐 ,𝑖𝑛𝑡 ,𝑖 (𝑡 ) + 𝐺 𝑐 ,𝑃𝐿 (𝑡 )3 

> 𝑖 =1

. (2)

Decreases in Gc,int,i and Gc,PL are modeled in terms of 

molecular degradation processes occurring in the EVA and are 

related to deacetylation, hydrolytic depolymerization, and bond 

scission associated with midchain radical generation and ß -

scission . The se three chemical degradation proc esses are 

described with four rate coefficients – k1 for deacetylation , k2

for hydrolytic depolymerization, and k3 and k4 for bond scission 

which consists of midchain radical generation (tied to k3) and 

ß-scission (tied to k4).

Gc,int,i over the course of exposure is related to the 

degradation rate as shown in (3), where Gc,int,i (0) is the initial 

adhesion energy for a given process, ai is an inten sity factory, 

and bi(t) to bi(0) are the number of remaining bonds and the 

initial number of bonds susceptible to rupture , respectively ; t he 

bond ratio is a function of the rate coefficients discussed above .

Gc,PL is proportional to Gc,int,i as shown in (4). The 

proportionality factor , d, at time t was approximated to be ( 5), 

incorporating a simplifying assumption where d decrease s

linearly with time. d(0) is a nonadjustable parameter that is a 

function of other input parameters , and t ∞ is the time at which 

the Gc,PL contribution is negligible with respect to the initial 

value (~30 years).

IV. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION 

A. Field and Accelerated Testing 

Fig. 1 juxtaposes the measured Colorado ( CO ) data with the 

model predictions specifically tuned with the 6 - and 12 -month 

data and a 16 -year datum from Colorado [10] . When the 

original model was published, the reference data available 

through 12 months and beyond 15 years validated the model’s 

predictive capabilities. Other worldwide adhesion data largely 

supported the overall long -term trends [10] . However, within 

the current work that extends further into the intermediate 

exposure range (>1 year), a large devi ation emerges at 46 

months between the measured (1510 J/m 2) and predicted value 

(~840 J/m 2). 

In Fig. 2, beginning with an initial Gc of 1800 +/ - 80 J/m 2, the 

data up through 12 months demonstrate a decreasing trend. 

However, from 12 to 68 months of exposure for these 3 outdoor 

locations, a general plateauing of Gc emerges. After 68 months ,

the adhesion energies are 67% and 80% of initial for the 

Arizona (AZ ) and Florida ( FL ) specimens , respectively, and Pursuant to the DOE Public Access Plan, this document represents the authors' peer-reviewed, accepted manuscript. The published version of the article is available from the relevant publisher. Fig. 1 Measured adhesion energy from CO mini -modules in comparison with     

> predicted values from a model originally tuned with the 6 -and 12 -month and
> 16 -year values (solid circles) . The model predicted a steadily decreasing trend
> after the initial year –a trend not followed by the subsequently measured data
> (open circles). Other prior worldwide adhesion data included for reference.

after 46 months, the adhesion energy is about 85% of the initial 

value for the CO specimen. 

Additionally, the existence of a plateau during the 

intermediate exposure times is corroborated by the accelerated 

aging data. Fig. 3 includes adhesion data for mini -modules aged 

for 10,000 hours in both ‘ligh t’ and ‘dark ’ conditions . The 

results from ‘light’ exposures, which include the same primary 

stressors as field exposures (heat, humidity, and UV radiation), 

display a similar drop in the early onset of aging before 

analogously plateauing at around 1550 J/ m2 up through 5,000 

hours. By 10,000 hours, however, the adhesion energy 

considerably drops to 240 J/m 2, which is 14% of initial 

adhesion. This value is much closer in magnitude to the 

threshold value of ~160 J/m 2 below which delamination failure 

is more l ikely to occur [8] . Additionally, this large drop in 

adhesio n energy is accompanied by a simultaneous shift in 

failure interface from the EVA/cell to glass/EVA interface – a

shift seen previously in fielded modules after significant 

degradation/exposure [9], [12] . In ‘dark’ conditions, without 

the UV radiation to help facilitate adhesive degradation, it takes 

longer for the adhesion to experience that initial drop, and then 

it plateaus around 1600 J/m 2 through 10,000 hours. 

Under ‘light’ conditions, the UV dosage produces 

approximately a 5.6X acceleration factor, considering the 

average UV light for a fixed plate module is about 0.25 UV 

suns. Then, assuming the sample temperature to be about 30˚C 

higher than the average effective temperature of a fielded 

module, which accelerates the UV p rocesses by 1.4x for every 

10˚C, the overall acceleration is roughly calculated to be 15.4X 

(see Table I) . Based on this acceleration factor, 5,000 hours in 

‘light’ conditions approximates 8.5 years in the field, and 

10,000 hours approximates 17 years. Thu s, t he large drop in 

adhesion between 5,000 and 10,000 hours parallels what is 

often seen in field aged modules , including in those discussed 

above and in [10] . Furthermore, based on the se accelerated 

results, the plateau in the field aged modules could potentially 

continue for at least a few more years beyond the ~6 years                

> Fig. 2Adhesion energy results for mini -modules aged outdoors in CO (blue),
> AZ (orange), and FL (grey) through up to 68 months of exposure. Over the first
> 12 months, adhesion decreases by 15 -20% , but from 12 through 68 months, the
> adhesion energy values generally level off .Original and revised predictions are
> superimposed on the data. Guide lines included to show plateauing trends.
> Fig. 3Adhesion energy results for mini -modules aged in environmental
> chambers under ‘light’ and ‘dark’ accelerated conditions. For ‘light’ conditions,
> similar to outdoor results (see Fig. 2), a plateau through 5,000 hours emerges
> after an initial drop in adhesi on energy, which is followed by a large decrease
> measured at 10,000 hours. Guide lines included to show general trends.

explored in this study. Moreover, the alignment of accelerated 

data with their fielded counterparts supports the use of 

accelerated agin g studies to probe these intermediate trends that 

are often challenging and time -consuming to address through 

fielded explorations. Pursuant to the DOE Public Access Plan, this document represents the authors' peer-reviewed, accepted manuscript. The published version of the article is available from the relevant publisher. TABLE I 

ACCELERATION FACTOR CALCULATION 

Accelerat ion 

> UV 0.25 UV suns 1.4 UV suns 5.6x
> Heat 55˚C 85˚C 2.7x
> Combined ----15.4x

B. Model Refinements 

Within the intermediate region, where the predicted adhesion 

deviates from measured data, the critical factors that affect the 

model prediction are the rate of UV -radical formation and 

subsequent ß -scission, the rate and acceleration of hydrolytic 

depolymerizat ion, and the plasticity contribution. The recent 

intermediate data enable refinement of the parameters as 

described below. Since the combination of the UV -radical 

formation and ß -scission reactions tend to dominate over the 

other reactions during this peri od [10], [14] , the current results 

suggest that the rate of these reactions is slower than originally 

presumed. Thus, a decrease in the associated rate coefficients 

helps delay the predicted adhesion loss – a 30% decrease is 

included in this revised model. This delay was also accounted 

for by decreasing the intensity factor a3 from 1 to 0.75 and 

doubling the intensity factor for deacetylation, a1, (in order to 

preserve the early adhesion drop ). R ecent work reveal s a

cataly tic interaction between acetate salt buildup at the 

glass/EVA interface that would accelerate the normally very 

slow process of hydrolytic depolymerization [14] . The rate 

coefficient for hydrolytic depolymerization originally included 

a linear acceleration profile, but the results in [14] suggest that 

the acceleration profil e should exhibit minimal change early on 

when the buildup of deacetylation pr oducts is low before more 

rapidly accelerating in the intermediate to late time frame as 

acetate species become more concentrated at the interface. 

Further insight into the contribution from this mechanism 

suggests that on account of the covalent siloxane bonds at the 

interface, it should have a greater initial adhesion energy ,

Gc,int,2 (0) [15] – here, 50% of the initial intrinsic Gc instead of 

30% (with the initial Gc values from the other intrinsic 

processes reduced each by 10% to maintain the same overall 

percentage ). A summary of original and refined model 

parameters is included in Table I I.

Most importantly, the plateau that emerge s through ~6 years 

reveal s that adjustments to the reaction rates alone can not 

account for the leveling off of adhesion. More accurate 

predict ions require larger changes that can only arise fro m the 

plasticity contributions to G c. These can most effectively be 

addressed using the proportionality factor d, discussed in 

section III . The linearly decreasing nature of d initially 

employed is revised to reflect the plateauing in G c following an 

initial decrease and the subsequent significant decrease beyond 

~6 years that is observed. These changes are better reflected 

using an exponential function, as shown below: 

𝑑 (𝑡 ) = 𝑑 (0) ∗ exp ( 𝑡 

> 𝑡 ∞

). (5)   

> Fig. 4The original and revised lifetime adhesion predictions alongside field
> data over 30 years of exposure. The revised prediction captures the general data
> trends throughout early, intermediate, and long -term exposure periods.

The revised and original predic tions through 10 years are 

superimposed on the field data in Fig. 2. The predicted adhesion 

loss now captures the plateauing trend observed in the 

intermediate region. Importantly, the prediction over the entire 

solar module lifetime (~30 years) demonstrat es that with these 

refinements, the model is still capable of capturing adhesion 

trends in the early and late periods of exposure as shown in Fig. 

4. A few of the original data measured on modules from various 

worldwide locations deviate from the expected trends (such as 

the low adhesion data at ~6 and ~11 years and those at ~27 

years), but this is to be expected on account of these assorted 

modules having sometimes drastically differing bills of 

materials and the possibility for manufacturing/assembly 

defe cts and other material variations . Notably, the overall shape 

of the prediction aligns with the general trends present in both 

field and accelerated aging data, and the model remains 

sensitive and tunable to the location -specific environmental 

differences that would affect degradation rates. 

V. OUTLOOK AND CONCLUSION 

Through both ~6 years of outdoor aging in 3 different US 

locations and 5,000 hours of accelerated ‘light’ aging, a distinct 

plateau region forms following an initial drop in adhesion. 

Sometime bet ween 5,000 and 10,000 hours of ‘light’ aging and 

after ~6 years of field aging, adhesion energy drops to values 

an order of magnitude lower. The agreement between field and 

accelerated adhesion data demonstrates the effectivity and 

potential of utilizing a ccelerated testing schemes to investigate

longer -term trends much more quickly. Previous adhesive 

modeling predicted that the adhesion energy would 

continuously decrease , which appeared accurate without 

inclusion of these recent intermediate data. 

Th ese results in combination with a powerful and fundamental 

predictive model enable a greater understand ing of the 

evolution and nature of adhesive degradation within EVA. By 

addressing the principal factors that affect the model prediction 

during the intermediate exposure time frame – the rate of UV -

radical formation and subsequent ß -scission, the rate and 

acceleration of hydrolytic depolymerization, and the plasticity 

contribution – the refined model can now accurately capture 

adhesion trends throughout the entirety of a module’s lifetime. 

Importantly, this modeling framework and approach are 

applicable to encapsulants beyond EVA since the degradation 

mechanisms relevant to any given encapsulant can be 

substituted and incorporated into the model. Thus, t he insights 

provided through this inve stigation not only highlight the 

importance of studying intermediate adhesion trends but will 

also prove invaluable to modeling the adhesive properties and 

degradation of other solar encapsulants .

ACKNOWLEDGMENT 

We thank Mike Kempe at NREL for his insight regarding 

estimation of acceleration factors. The views expressed in the 

article do not necessarily represent the views of the DOE or the 

U.S. Government. The U.S. Government retains and the 

publisher, by accepting the article for publication, acknowledges 

that the U.S. Government retains a nonexclusive, paid -up, 

irrevocable, worldwide license to publish or reproduce the 

published form of this work, or allow others to do so, for U.S. 

Government purposes. 

REFERENCES 

[1] A. W. Czanderna and F. J. Pern, “Encapsulation of PV modules 

using ethylene vinyl acetate copolymer as a pottant: A critical 

review,” Sol. Energy Mater. Sol. Cells , vol. 43, no. 2, pp. 101 –181, 

Sep. 1996, doi: 10.1016/0927 -0248(95)00150 -6. 

[2] M. D. Kempe, G. J. Jorgensen, K. M. Terwilliger, T. J. McMahon, 

C. E. Kennedy, and T. T. Borek, “Acetic acid production and glass 

transition concerns with ethylene -vinyl acetate used in photovoltaic

Source title: Influence of environmental conditions on dentin bond strengths of ...
Source type: web
Content:
Influence of environmental conditions on dentin bond strengths of one-application adhesive systems - PMID: 15470878 Influence of environmental conditions on dentin bond strengths of one-application adhesive systems Abstract Resin composites are considered susceptible to environmental conditions that might affect bond strength. This study investigated the influence of relative humidity and temperature on the dentin bond strength of newly developed one-application adhesive systems. Bonding systems employed in this study were five commercial one-application adhesive systems. Labial surfaces of bovine incisors were ground wet on 600-grit SiC paper. The teeth were transferred to a controlled temperature and humidity chamber and the specimens were prepared in six different environmental conditions, A) 25 +/- 0.5 degrees C, 50 +/- 5% RH, B) 25 +/- 0.5 degrees C, 80 +/- 5% RH, C) 25 +/- 0.5 degrees C, 95 +/- 5% RH, D) 37 +/- 0.5 degrees C, 50 +/- 5% RH, E) 37 +/- 0.5 degrees C, 80 +/- 5% RH, F) 37 +/- 0.5 degrees C, 95 +/- 5% RH. The dentin surfaces were treated according to each manufacturer's instructions. Resin composites of each bonding system were condensed into a mold and light irradiated. After storage in 37 degrees C water for 24 hours, 10 specimens per group were tested in a shear mode in a testing machine at a crosshead speed of 1.0 mm/minute. Statistical analysis was carried out with one-way ANOVA followed by Duncan's multiple range tests at a p-value of 0.05. The dentin bond strengths of one-application adhesive systems decreased with increasing RH. These data suggest that the relative high humidity in the oral environment needs consideration in developing clinical procedures for management of these one-application adhesive systems.
